title: The Future of the World’s Language

passages.title: The Future of the World’s Language
passages.source_html:
The Future of the World’s Language 


A 


Of the world’s 6,500 living languages, around half are expected to the out by the end of this century, according to UNESCO. Just 11 are spoken by more than half of the earth’s population, so it is little wonder that those used by only a few are being left behind as we become a more homogenous, global society. In short, 95 percent of the world’s languages are spoken by only five percent of its population – a remarkable level of linguistic diversity stored in tiny pockets of speakers around the world. Mark Turin, a university professor, has launched WOLP (World Oral Language Project) to prevent the language from the brink of extinction. 


B 


He is trying to encourage indigenous communities to collaborate with anthropologists around the world to record what he calls ‘oral literature’ through video cameras, voice recorders and other multimedia tools by awarding grants from a £30,000 pot that the project has secured this year. The idea is to collate this literature in a digital archive that can be accessed on demand and will make the nuts and bolts of lost cultures readily available. 


C 


For many of these communities, the oral tradition is at the heart of their culture. The stories they tell are creative as well as communicative. Unlike the languages with celebrated written traditions, such as Sanskrit, Hebrew and Ancient Greek, few indigenous communities have recorded their own languages or ever had them recorded until now. 


D

 

The project suggested itself when Turin was teaching in Nepal. He wanted to study for a PhD in endangered languages and, while discussing it with his professor at Leiden University in the Netherlands, was drawn to a map on his tutor’s wall. The map was full of pins of a variety of colours which represented all the world’s languages that were completely undocumented. At random, Turin chose a ‘pin’ to document. It happened to belong to the Thangmi tribe, an indigenous community in the hills east of Kathmandu, the capital of Nepal. ‘Many of the choices anthropologists and linguists who work on these traditional field-work projects are quite random,’ he admits. 


E 


Continuing his work with the Thangmi community in the 1990s, Turin began to record the language he was hearing, realising that not only was this language and its culture entirely undocumented, it was known to few outside the tiny community. He set about trying to record their language and myth of origins. ‘I wrote 1,000 pages of grammar in English that nobody could use – but I realised that wasn’t enough. It wasn’t enough for me, it wasn’t enough for them. It simply wasn’t going to work as something for the community. So then I produced this trilingual word list in Thangmi, Nepali and English.’ 


F 


In short, it was the first ever publication of that language. That small dictionary is still sold in local schools for a modest 20 rupees, and used as part of a wider cultural regeneration process to educate children about their heritage and language. The task is no small undertaking: Nepal itself is a country of massive ethnic and linguistic diversity, home to 100 languages from four different language families. What’s more, even fewer ethnic Thangmi speak the Thangmi language. Many of the community members have taken to speaking Nepali, the national language taught in schools and spread through the media, and community elders are dying without passing on their knowledge. 


G 


Despite Turin’s enthusiasm for his subject, he is baffled by many linguists’ refusal to engage in the issue he is working on. ‘Of the 6,500 languages spoken on Earth, many do not have written traditions and many of these spoken forms are endangered,’ he says, ‘There are more linguists in universities around the world than there are spoken languages – but most of them aren’t working on this issue. To me it’s amazing that in this day and age, we still have an entirely incomplete image of the world’s linguistic diversity. People do PhDs on the apostrophe in French, yet we still don’t know how many languages are spoken.’ 


H 


‘When a language becomes endangered, so too does a cultural world view. We want to engage with indigenous people to document their myths and folklore, which can be harder to find funding for if you are based outside Western universities.’ 


I 


Yet, despite the struggles facing initiatives such as the World Oral Literature Project, there are historical examples that point to the possibility that language restoration is no mere academic pipe dream. The revival of a modern form of Hebrew in the 19th century is often cited as one of the best proofs that languages long dead, belonging to small communities, can be resurrected and embraced by a large number of people. By the 20th century, Hebrew was well on its way to becoming the main language of the Jewish population of both Ottoman and British Palestine. It is now spoken by more than seven million people in Israel. 


J 


Yet, despite the difficulties these communities face in saving their languages, Dr Turin believes that the fate of the world’s endangered languages is not sealed, and globalisation is not necessarily the nefarious perpetrator of evil it is often presented to be. ‘I call it the globalisation paradox: on the one hand globalisation and rapid socio-economic change are the things that are eroding and challenging diversity. But on the other, globalisation is providing us with new and very exciting tools and facilities to get to places to document those things that globalisation is eroding. Also, the communities at the coal-face of change are excited by what globalisation has to offer.’ 


K 


In the meantime, the race is on to collect and protect as many of the languages as possible, so that the Rai Shaman in eastern Nepal and those in the generations that follow him can continue their traditions and have a sense of identity. And it certainly is a race: Turin knows his project’s limits and believes it inevitable that a large number of those languages will disappear. ‘We have to be wholly realistic. A project like ours is in no position, and was not designed, to keep languages alive. The only people who can help languages survive are the people in those communities themselves. They need to be reminded that it’s good to speak their own language and I think we can help them do that – becoming modem doesn’t mean you have to lose your language.’

groups.title: Questions 27-40
groups.source_html:
Questions 27-31 


Complete the summary using the list of words, A-G, below. Write your answers in boxes 27-31 on your answer sheet.


Options (Word Bank):
A. similarity
B. significance
C. funding
D. minority
E. education
F. difference
G. language
H. diversity
I. majority
J. disappearance
27

Of the world's 6,500 living languages, about half of them are expected to be extinct. Most of the world’s languages are spoken by a 27  of people.


正确答案： D

原文答案句：Of the world’s 6,500 living languages, around half are expected to the out by the end of this century, according to UNESCO. Just 11 are spoken by more than half of the earth’s population, so it is little wonder that those used by only a few are being left behind as we become a more homogenous, global society. In short, 95 percent of the world’s languages are spoken by only five percent of its population – a remarkable level of linguistic diversity stored in tiny pockets of speakers around the world.

对应段落：A段

审题：题目问的是“世界上大多数语言是由百分之多少的人口使用的？”

定位关键信息：利用题目的单词“world’s languages”和“spoken by”定位到A段中“95 percent of the world’s languages are spoken by only five percent of its population”这句话。

解题思路：根据原文中的这句话可以确定，世界上95%的语言是由全球人口的5%使用的，因此可以得出答案为“D”，即“少数”。


28

However, Professor Turin set up a project WOLP to prevent 28  of the languages.

正确答案： J

原文答案句：Mark Turin, a university professor, has launched WOLP (World Oral Language Project) to prevent the language from the brink of extinction.

对应段落：A段

审题：题目问的是Turin教授设立WOLP项目的目的是什么。

定位关键信息：题目中的"Professor Turin"和"project WOLP"可以帮助定位到A段中的相关句子。

解题思路：根据A段中的原文句子，Turin教授设立WOLP项目的目的是为了防止语言濒临灭绝，因此答案为"extinction"。



29

The project provides the community with 29  to enable people to record their endangered languages.

正确答案： C

原文答案句：He is trying to encourage indigenous communities to collaborate with anthropologists around the world to record what he calls ‘oral literature’ through video cameras, voice recorders and other multimedia tools by awarding grants from a £30,000 pot that the project has secured this year.

对应段落：B段  

审题：题目问的是项目为社区提供了什么。  

定位关键信息：根据题目中的"the project provides the community with"和"to record"定位到B段  

解题思路：原文提到"He is trying to encourage indigenous communities to collaborate with anthropologists around the world to record what he calls ‘oral literature’ through video cameras, voice recorders and other multimedia tools by awarding grants"，说明项目通过awarding给community提供to record的东西。根据选项列表，C选项"funding"最符合原文描述。



30

The oral tradition has great cultural 30  .

正确答案： B

原文答案句：For many of these communities, the oral tradition is at the heart of their culture.

对应段落：C段

审题：题目问的是“口头传统具有巨大的文化的什么”。

定位关键信息：通过题干中的“oral tradition”和“cultural”这两个关键词可以定位到C段。

解题思路：原文中提到，口头传统是许多社区文化的核心，具有重要的文化意义。因此，答案为B。



31

An important 31  between languages spoken by few people and languages with celebrated written documents existed in many communities.

正确答案： F

原文答案句：Unlike the languages with celebrated written traditions, such as Sanskrit, Hebrew and Ancient Greek, few indigenous communities have recorded their own languages or ever had them recorded until now.

对应段落：C段

审题：题目问的是在许多社区中，使用人数少的语言和使用有悠久书面传统的语言之间存在一个重要的什么差异。

定位关键信息：通过题目中的“languages spoken by few people”和“languages with celebrated written documents”可以定位到C段中“Unlike the languages with celebrated written traditions, such as Sanskrit, Hebrew and Ancient Greek, few indigenous communities have recorded their own languages or ever had them recorded until now.”这句话。

解题思路：原文中明确提到了使用人数少的语言和使用有悠久书面传统的语言之间的差异，即前者很少有社区记录自己的语言，而后者则有悠久的历史记录。所以两者存在差异，因此答案为F。




Questions 32-35 


Do the following statements agree with the information given in the passage? In boxes 32-35 on your answer sheet, write TRUE if the statement is true FALSE if the statement is false NOT GIVEN if the information is not given in the passage


32

Turin argued that anthropologists and linguists usually think carefully before selecting an area to research.

正确答案： B. FALSE

原文答案句：Many of the choices anthropologists and linguists who work on these traditional field-work projects are quite random, he admits.

对应段落：D段

审题：题目问的是Turin是否认为人类学家和语言学家在选择研究领域时通常会仔细考虑。

定位关键信息：利用题干中的"Turin argued that anthropologists and linguists usually think carefully before selecting an area to research"中的关键词"Turin"、"anthropologists and linguists"、"think carefully before selecting an area to research"来定位。

解题思路：根据定位到的D段原文句子"Many of the choices anthropologists and linguists who work on these traditional field-work projects are quite random, he admits."可以看出，Turin承认人类学家和语言学家在选择研究领域时通常是随机的，而不是仔细考虑的。因此，题干中的说法与原文信息相反，答案为FALSE。


33

Turin concluded that the Thangmi language had few similarities with other languages.

正确答案： C. NOT GIVEN

原文答案句：Unlike the languages with celebrated written traditions, such as Sanskrit, Hebrew and Ancient Greek, few indigenous communities have recorded their own languages or ever had them recorded until now.

对应段落：C段

审题：题目问的是Turin是否得出结论，认为Thangmi语言与其他语言很少有相似之处。

定位关键信息：通过题目中的"Turin"和"Thangmi language"可以定位到C段，该段提到了Turin对Thangmi语言的研究背景。

解题思路：原文中C段提到的是少数土著社区的语言记录情况，并未提到Thangmi语言与其他语言的相似性或差异性。题目中的结论在原文中并未提及，因此无法判断其真假。


34

Turin has written that 1000-page document was inappropriate for Thangmi community.

正确答案： A. TRUE

原文答案句：I wrote 1,000 pages of grammar in English that nobody could use – but I realised that wasn’t enough. It wasn’t enough for me, it wasn’t enough for them. It simply wasn’t going to work as something for the community.

对应段落：E段

审题：题目问Turin表示1000页文档不适合Thangmi社区。

定位关键信息：通过题目中的"Turin"、"1000-page document"和"Thangmi community"定位到E段，找到Turin描述他写文档的内容。

解题思路：原文中Turin明确提到他写的1000页语法文档是英文的，没有人能用，对社区也不适用，直接对应题目中的"was inappropriate for Thangmi community"，因此判断为TRUE。


35

Some Nepalese schools lack resources to devote to language teaching.

正确答案： C. NOT GIVEN

原文答案句：The small dictionary is still sold in local schools for a modest 20 rupees, and used as part of a wider cultural regeneration process to educate children about their heritage and language.

对应段落：F段

审题：题目问“一些尼泊尔学校缺乏资源用于语言教学”是否与文章信息一致。

定位关键信息：利用关键词“Nepalese schools”和“lack resources”定位到F段，该段提到学校使用小词典进行文化复兴和教育，但未提及资源缺乏。

解题思路：F段提到学校使用小词典（售价20卢比）进行语言教育。题目说学校缺乏资源，与原文信息无关联，因此答案为NOT GIVEN。



Questions 36-40 


Choose the correct letter, A, B, C or D. Write the correct letter in boxes 36-40 on your answer sheet.


36

Why does Turin say people do PhDs on the apostrophe in French?

正确答案： A. He believes that researchers have limited role in the research of languages.

原文答案句：There are more linguists in universities around the world than there are spoken languages – but most of them aren’t working on this issue. To me it’s amazing that in this day and age, we still have an entirely incomplete image of the world’s linguistic diversity. People do PhDs on the apostrophe in French, yet we still don’t know how many languages are spoken.

对应段落：G段

审题：题目问的是Turin为什么说人们研究法语中的撇号（apostrophe）来攻读博士学位。

定位关键信息：利用题目中的“apostrophe in French”定位到G段末句，找到包含这一信息的句子。

解题思路：根据原文中的句子“People do PhDs on the apostrophe in French, yet we still don’t know how many languages are spoken.”可以推断，Turin认为人们研究法语中的撇号这种细枝末节的问题，而忽视了研究世界上有多少种语言这种更宏观、更重要的语言问题。这表明他认为研究人员在语言研究中的作用有限，他们应该关注更重要的语言问题，而不是像研究撇号这样无关紧要的细节。因此，选项A“他认为研究人员在语言研究中的作用有限”最符合原文意思。


37

What is discussed in the Paragraph I?

正确答案： A. Forces driving people to believe endangered languages can survive.

原文答案句：The revival of a modern form of Hebrew in the 19th century is often cited as one of the best proofs that languages long dead, belonging to small communities, can be resurrected and embraced by a large number of people.  

对应段落：I段  

审题：题目问第九段讨论了什么内容。  

定位关键信息：根据题目中的"ninth paragraph"定位到I段，该段主要讲述了希伯来语的复兴作为语言恢复的例子。  

解题思路：I段通过希伯来语在19世纪的复兴，说明曾经消亡的语言可以复活并被广泛使用，这证明了濒危语言有生存的可能。因此，选项A"促使人们相信濒危语言可以生存的驱动力"与段落内容一致。其他选项B、C、D均未在段落中提及。


38

How is the WOLP’s prospect?

正确答案： D. It has chance to succeed to protect the engendered languages.

原文答案句：Turin knows his project’s limits and believes it inevitable that a large number of those languages will disappear. ‘We have to be wholly realistic. A project like ours is in no position, and was not designed, to keep languages alive. The only people who can help languages survive are the people in those communities themselves. They need to be reminded that it’s good to speak their own language and I think we can help them do that – becoming modem doesn’t mean you have to lose your language.’

对应段落：K段

审题：题目问的是WOLP的前景如何。

定位关键信息：通过题目中的关键词"WOLP's prospect"可以定位到文章中关于WOLP前景的描述。

解题思路：根据原文答案句，Dr Turin认为这个项目并不是让所有的语言都存活但是可以帮助人们去保护语言。这意味着WOLP有可能成功保护濒危语言，因此D选项正确。


39

What is Turin’s main point of globalisation?

正确答案： B. Globalisation has both advantages and disadvantages.

原文答案句Dr Turin believes that the fate of the world’s endangered languages is not sealed, and globalisation is not necessarily the nefarious perpetrator of evil it is often presented to be. ‘I call it the globalisation paradox: on the one hand globalisation and rapid socio-economic change are the things that are eroding and challenging diversity. But on the other, globalisation is providing us with new and very exciting tools and facilities to get to places to document those things that globalisation is eroding.

对应段落：J段

审题：题目问的是Turin对全球化的主要观点是什么。

定位关键信息：利用题目的关键词"Turin’s main point of globalisation"定位到文章中J段，该段主要讨论了Turin对全球化的看法。

解题思路：原文中J段明确提到Turin认为全球化是一个悖论，它既侵蚀和挑战多样性，又提供了新的工具和设施来记录那些正在被全球化侵蚀的事物。这与选项B"全球化既有优势也有劣势"完全一致。选项A、C和D都没有在原文中明确提及，因此可以排除。


40

What does Turin suggest that community people should do?

正确答案： C. Keep up with the modern society without losing their language.

原文答案句：The only people who can help languages survive are the people in those communities themselves. They need to be reminded that it’s good to speak their own language and I think we can help them do that – becoming modem doesn’t mean you have to lose your language.

对应段落：K段

审题：题目问Turin建议社区的人们应该做什么。

定位关键信息：通过题目的关键词"Turin suggest that community people should do"可以定位到K段中Turin的话。

解题思路：K段中Turin提到，只有社区的人们自己才能帮助语言生存，他们需要被提醒说自己的语言是好的，并且他认为他们可以帮助社区的人们做到这一点——变得现代化并不意味着你必须失去你的语言。这与选项C"跟上现代社会而不失去他们的语言"完全一致。因此，答案是C。
